I had plans for a simple meal tonight, but now everything I could make seems bland compared to whatever they're doing over there.
reactions: Tomorrow I'm using twice the garlic I normally would. I refuse to be outdone by a stranger's kitchen.

entries.intro: I broke a shoelace this morning and had to tie the two halves together. It's holding, barely.
details: The knot's right in the middle where the boot bends. I can feel it with every step.
It's the kind of fix that'll last just long enough for me to forget about it, then fail at the worst time.
reactions: Temporary solutions have a way of becoming permanent if you're lazy enough.

entries.intro: I dreamed about a house I've never been in, but it felt completely familiar.
details: I knew where the rooms were, where the light came through. Even the smell of it.
I woke up missing a place that doesn't exist. That's a strange kind of homesickness.
reactions: Dreams give you memories of things that never happened. I don't know what to do with that.

entries.intro: I spent too long staring at the fire tonight. Now everywhere I look there's a green spot.
details: It floats in the center of my vision. Follows me everywhere: door, window, ceiling.
I know it'll fade, but right now the world has a permanent green decoration I didn't ask for.
reactions: Fire is mesmerizing right up until it blinds you a little. Then it's just regret.

statCheck.pass: Bright light bleaches the photoreceptors temporarily. The afterimage is the opposite color of what you stared at: fire is orange, so you see blue-green.
statCheck.fail: I don't know why it does that. Just know I should look away sooner.

entries.intro: My socks got wet through my boots and nothing is worse than wet socks.
details: That cold, squelching feeling with every step. Like your feet are slowly being claimed by a puddle.
I changed them, and the dry pair lasted about twenty minutes before the boots soaked through again.
reactions: I need better boots. Or better socks. Or better weather. Any of the three.

entries.intro: I counted how many times I opened and closed the same drawer today. Nine. Nine times.
details: I kept going to it for something, forgetting what, closing it, remembering, and going back.
By the sixth time I started laughing at myself. By the ninth I was just opening it out of spite, like I could force my brain to cooperate.
reactions: That drawer and I had a very busy relationship today.

entries.intro: I woke up to absolute silence this morning. Not even wind. Just stillness.
details: It was so quiet my ears started making their own noise to compensate. That faint high ringing.
I lay there for a while just being in it. Real quiet is rarer than you'd think.
reactions: Silence isn't the absence of sound. It's the presence of calm.

entries.intro: I picked up a stick on my walk today. No reason. Just a good stick.
details: The right weight, the right length. It felt good in the hand.
I carried it the whole way home and now it's leaning by the door. No purpose for it. Just liked it.
reactions: We never really stop collecting things. The reasons just get vaguer.

entries.intro: My neighbor waved at me today from across the way. Just a simple wave. Made my whole morning.
details: We don't talk much. Different schedules, different lives. But the wave was enough.
It said 'I see you, I know you're there.' That's all anyone needs sometimes.
reactions: I'll wave first tomorrow. Pass it along.

entries.intro: I finally ate the last of the preserves I put up last season. The jar's been staring at me for months.
details: I kept saving them for a special occasion. Then I realized no occasion was coming, so I just opened them.
They were perfect. Would've been a shame to let them go another month.
reactions: Stop saving things for later. 'Now' is the occasion.

entries.intro: I lay in bed this morning listening to the rain and decided today could wait.
details: Everything on the list is still on the list. The rain didn't wash any of it away.
But I got an extra hour of warmth and I regret nothing.
reactions: There are some mornings where staying put is the wisest thing you can do.

entries.intro: I've been waking up with a stiff jaw lately. Must be clenching it in my sleep.
details: By midday it loosens up, but those first few hours I feel like I've been chewing rocks all night.
I didn't even know you could tense your jaw while unconscious. The body does what it wants, apparently.
reactions: I'm sure it'll pass once whatever's bothering me sorts itself out.

statCheck.pass: I think it's tension I'm carrying from the day. The jaw holds more stress than you'd expect.
statCheck.fail: No idea why it happens. Just woke up that way.

entries.intro: Washed every dish in the house this morning. Even the ones in the back of the shelf I never use.
details: There's something about hot water and a rhythm that clears your head. I wasn't even thinking about anything by the end.
My hands are pruned and wrinkled now, but the kitchen looks immaculate.
reactions: Probably the most productive I'll be all day, and it was just dishes.

entries.intro: I noticed the door to the pantry doesn't quite close anymore. It catches on the frame.
details: I have to lift it slightly and push with my hip. It's become a two-handed operation just to get to the flour.
The wood must have swollen with the damp. Or settled. Something shifted, anyway.
reactions: I'll shave the edge down one of these days. For now, the hip technique works.

entries.intro: The bread didn't rise properly this morning. Flat as a plank.
details: I followed the same steps I always do. Same amounts, same timing. Sometimes it just decides not to cooperate.
I ate it anyway. Flat bread is still bread.
reactions: Tomorrow's batch will probably be fine. Baking is temperamental like that.

statCheck.pass: The yeast was probably too old. It loses strength if you keep it past a certain point, even if it looks fine.
statCheck.fail: I've never understood why it works some days and not others.

entries.intro: I stood in the doorway for a solid minute this morning trying to remember what I came outside for.
details: The thought was right there when I stood up from the chair. Gone by the time I reached the door.
I went back inside, sat down in the same spot, and it came back immediately. Water. I needed water.
reactions: The mind has its own schedule, and it doesn't consult me.

entries.intro: My hip's been clicking when I climb stairs. Not painful, just loud enough that I notice.
details: It started a few weeks ago and hasn't gotten worse. But it hasn't stopped either.
I've started taking the steps slower, which I suppose was coming anyway.
reactions: Another thing to add to the list of noises my body makes now.

statCheck.pass: It's the joint catching where it didn't used to. If you keep it moving, it doesn't lock up as often.
statCheck.fail: Bodies click. I try not to think about it.

entries.intro: It rained last night and the air this morning was perfect. Clean and cool and quiet.
details: The ground was still damp when I stepped out. Everything had that just-washed look to it.
Even the birds sounded different. Louder, maybe, or just happier.
reactions: Days that start like that usually turn out well. Or at least they feel like they should.

entries.intro: I found a beetle in my boot this morning. Just sitting in there like it was home.
details: Good thing I checked before putting it on. I usually don't, but something felt off.
I set it outside and it walked away without a care. Bold little thing.
reactions: I'll be shaking my boots out every morning now, I expect.

entries.intro: I finished a chore I'd been avoiding for a month and the relief is absurd.
details: It took maybe half an hour. Half an hour, after a month of dreading it.
I think I spent more energy avoiding it than actually doing it.
reactions: The dread is always worse than the task. I know this. I'll forget by next time.

entries.intro: Fog so thick this morning I couldn't see the path from my door. Just a wall of gray.
details: Sound travels strangely in fog. I could hear voices but couldn't tell which direction they came from.
By noon it had burned off completely, like it was never there.
reactions: Fog mornings always make the world feel smaller. Not in a bad way.

entries.intro: I've been sleeping with an extra blanket even though it's not that cold. Just feels right.
details: The weight of it is comforting more than the warmth. Like being held down in a good way.
I'll probably sweat through the night eventually, but right now it's perfect.
reactions: Sometimes comfort isn't about temperature. It's about feeling settled.

statCheck.pass: I think it's the weight that does it. It keeps you from tossing around, and you sleep deeper because of it.
statCheck.fail: I don't know why it helps. It just does.

entries.intro: I peeled a whole basket of root vegetables today and I can still feel it in my thumb.
details: The knife handle wears a groove into your skin after that many. Red and tender.
I've got enough peeled to last me the week, at least. That's the upside.
reactions: Cooking is mostly prep work. The actual cooking is the easy part.

entries.intro: Woke up absolutely freezing. The fire must have gone out sometime in the night.
details: I lay there for a while arguing with myself about getting up to relight it. The cold won eventually.
The coals were still faintly warm underneath the ash, so it didn't take long to get it going again.
reactions: That first blast of warmth when the fire catches again is worth the cold walk across the floor.

statCheck.pass: Banking the coals in ash before bed keeps them alive longer. A thick layer traps the heat and they'll hold through the night.
statCheck.fail: I don't know any trick to keep it going. The fire does what it does.

entries.intro: I've been meaning to write a letter for weeks. I know what I want to say, I just can't make myself start.
details: The parchment's been sitting on the table with the ink beside it. Ready to go. I walk past it every day.
It's not a difficult letter. Just a few words to someone I haven't spoken to in too long.
reactions: I'll do it tonight. I've said that before, but this time I mean it.

statCheck.pass: It's easier to keep putting it off than to admit how long it's been. The longer you wait, the harder the first line gets.
statCheck.fail: I'll get to it. Just not right now.

entries.intro: The wind changed direction overnight. You can feel it: the air's coming from somewhere different.
details: Yesterday it was warm and steady from the south. Now it's got that sharp edge that means the weather's turning.
The trees are all leaning the wrong way compared to yesterday. It's disorienting.
reactions: I don't need a weather vane. You can feel a shift like that in your skin.

entries.intro: I ate too much last night and paid for it all morning. Couldn't get comfortable no matter how I lay.
details: The food was good, that's the problem. I knew I should stop and I didn't.
My stomach made sounds I've never heard before. Impressive, in a terrible way.
reactions: I always say I'll learn, and I never do.

statCheck.pass: Mint leaves steeped in warm water settle the stomach faster than anything else I've tried.
statCheck.fail: I just wait it out. No cure for overindulgence except time.

entries.intro: The well rope frayed and I nearly lost the bucket this morning. Caught it just in time.
details: The whole rope is rough now that I look at it. Threads coming apart all the way up.
I need to replace it before it snaps for real. One more thing for the list.
reactions: At least I still have a bucket.

entries.intro: I had the best sleep I've had in months last night. Woke up feeling like a new person.
details: No tossing, no waking up at odd hours. Just solid, dreamless darkness and then morning.
I don't know what I did differently. If I could bottle it, I'd be rich.
reactions: The difference between a good night and a bad one is everything.

entries.intro: My ankle's been tender since I twisted it two days ago. Nothing serious, just annoying.
details: I stepped on a loose stone and it rolled under me. The kind of thing that happens when you're not paying attention.
I've been favoring the other leg, which means now both of them are tired.
reactions: It's healing. Slowly, but it's healing.

statCheck.pass: Cold water wraps for the first day, then warm after that. Alternating helps the swelling go down faster.
statCheck.fail: I just try to stay off it. Not much else you can do.

entries.intro: I caught myself talking to a plant today. Telling it to grow faster.
details: I don't think it's working, but I've been doing it all week and I'm committed now.
My grandmother used to do the same thing. Maybe it's hereditary.
reactions: If anyone overheard me, they were polite enough not to mention it.

entries.intro: I tried cutting my own hair this morning. Mistake.
details: The back is uneven and there's a patch near my ear that's shorter than I intended. Significantly shorter.
I figured: how hard can it be? The answer is: harder than it looks.
reactions: It'll grow back. In the meantime, I'm wearing a hat.

entries.intro: I scrubbed the stove top today until it gleamed. Took the better part of an hour.
details: The amount of grease that came off was frankly disgusting. I'd gotten used to it gradually.
Now it looks like it did when I first got it. Almost too clean to use.
reactions: Give it a week and it'll look exactly the same. But right now it's beautiful.

entries.intro: The afternoons have been warm enough to sit outside without a coat. Barely, but enough.
details: There's a window of about two hours where the sun is just right. After that the chill comes back.
I've been taking my midday meal outside during that window. It changes the whole feel of the day.
reactions: Small comforts like that keep the season bearable.

entries.intro: I sneezed four times in a row this morning. Right in the middle of breakfast.
details: Each one louder than the last. By the fourth, I'd knocked my spoon off the table.
I'm not getting sick: at least I don't think so. Just dust, probably.
reactions: At least nobody was watching. Dignified, it was not.

entries.intro: I've been going to bed earlier and earlier this week. Last night I was under the blankets before the sun had fully set.
details: It's not that I'm tired. I think I just run out of things to do once the light goes.
I lay there awake for a while, but there's something nice about being in bed with nowhere to be.
reactions: I used to think early bedtime was a defeat. Now it feels like common sense.

entries.intro: The bottom of my pot has gotten so black I can't scrub it off anymore. It's permanent.
details: I tried sand, I tried ash, I tried soaking it overnight. Nothing. That soot is there for good.
It still cooks fine, so I suppose it doesn't matter. But I know it's there.
reactions: A well-used pot looks like that. It's a sign it's doing its job.

statCheck.pass: A paste of ash and fat left on overnight actually cuts through most of it. But the deepest layer is baked in for good.
statCheck.fail: I've tried everything. That pot is just black now.

entries.intro: My right eye's been twitching on and off all day. Just the eyelid, but it's maddening.
details: It stops when I press on it and starts again the moment I let go.
I've been trying to ignore it, but it's like a heartbeat right at the corner of my vision.
reactions: It'll stop when it stops. Until then I'm just going to look slightly deranged.

entries.intro: First thunderstorm of the season came through last night. Rattled the whole house.
details: The lightning was close enough that there was no gap between the flash and the sound. Just one solid crash.
I checked for leaks this morning and found nothing. The roof held.
reactions: I like a good storm when I'm inside. There's something satisfying about being sheltered from it.

statCheck.pass: You can judge the distance by counting between flash and thunder. No gap means it's right overhead.
statCheck.fail: All I know is it was loud and close. That's enough for me.

entries.intro: I've been drinking more water lately. Not because anyone told me to: just thirsty.
details: Maybe it's the warmer days, or maybe I've been working harder without realizing it.
I refilled my cup four times before midday. That's unusual for me.
reactions: At least it's a harmless habit.

entries.intro: My fingernails are wrecked. Chipped, torn, dirt under every one of them.
details: I scrubbed them for ten minutes and they still look like I've been digging trenches. Which, to be fair, I was.
Some of them are down to the quick. The kind where everything stings for a day or two.
reactions: Gloves would help. I keep saying that and then never wearing them.

statCheck.pass: Rubbing them with oil before you work helps. The dirt can't get into the creases as deep if the skin's already coated.
statCheck.fail: I don't bother with prevention. Just scrub after and hope for the best.

entries.intro: I watched the sunset from the step tonight. The whole thing, start to finish.
details: I don't usually sit still that long. But the colors were changing so slowly it felt like I couldn't leave.
The last bit, right before it went dark, was this deep red that made everything look warm.
reactions: You forget how good it is to just watch something happen without needing to do anything about it.

entries.intro: I sorted through a box of old things today. Kept half, threw out the rest.
details: Some of it I didn't even recognize. Things I must have put away years ago and forgotten completely.
There's a strange guilt in throwing out things you don't need anymore. As if keeping them means something.
reactions: The box is lighter now. So am I, in a way.

entries.intro: I can't get warm today. Three layers and I'm still cold.
details: The fire's going, the door's shut, there's no draft I can find. Just cold from the inside out.
My hands are the worst. I've been sitting on them like a child.
reactions: Some days the cold just wins. You accept it and drink something hot.

statCheck.pass: When the cold comes from inside, it usually means you haven't eaten enough. The body needs fuel to burn for heat.
statCheck.fail: No idea why some days are worse than others. Just cold.

entries.intro: I patched a pair of trousers this afternoon. The knee had finally worn through.
details: The stitching isn't pretty, but it'll hold. I'm not entering any contests with it.
I've had these trousers longer than most of my friendships. They've earned a repair.
reactions: There's satisfaction in keeping something going past when most people would replace it.

entries.intro: I left the window open last night and the cold woke me up at some awful hour.
details: The room was freezing. I had to get up, close it, pile another blanket on, and then I couldn't fall back asleep.
I'd opened it because it was stuffy before bed. Now I remember why I usually leave it shut.
reactions: That's a mistake I'll make again next warm evening. I always do.

entries.intro: Everything I cooked today was slightly off. Not bad, just not right.
details: Even the {food_type} came out wrong, and that's the one thing I usually get right.
Some days your hands just don't cooperate with your intentions.
reactions: I ate it all anyway. Off is still food.

entries.intro: I woke up from a good dream and spent the whole morning trying to remember what it was.
details: I know it was good because I woke up smiling. But the details were gone before I opened my eyes.
All I've got is a feeling. Warm, familiar, like being somewhere safe.
reactions: Maybe it's better not remembering. The feeling was enough.

entries.intro: My neck is sunburned. Just the back, where I forgot to cover up.
details: It's that angry red that you know is going to peel in a couple of days.
Everything brushes against it: my collar, my hair, the wind. Each one a small reminder.
reactions: I know better. And yet, every year.

statCheck.pass: Aloe sap takes the sting out faster than anything. You just split a leaf and rub the gel on.
statCheck.fail: I don't know any remedy. Just wait and suffer.

entries.intro: I took the long walk this morning. The one that loops around and takes about an hour.
details: I wasn't planning to. Started out for a short stroll and kept finding reasons to go a little farther.
My legs are tired now, but the good kind of tired. The kind that means you actually moved.
reactions: I should do that more often. The world looks different at walking speed.

entries.intro: The chimney needs cleaning. I can tell by how the smoke hesitates before it goes up.
details: It's one of those jobs that's dirty, awkward, and miserable the whole time you're doing it.
I've been putting it off because I know what it involves. Soot everywhere, on everything.
reactions: Better to do it now than in the dead of winter when I actually need the fire.

statCheck.pass: If you let it build up too long, the creosote inside can catch fire itself. That's the real danger of a neglected chimney.
statCheck.fail: I just know it doesn't draw right when it's dirty.

entries.intro: I sliced onions this morning and my eyes are still burning two hours later.
details: I tried every trick: cold water, breathing through my mouth, cutting near the window. None of it helped.
By the end I couldn't see what I was cutting. Just aiming and hoping.
reactions: The stew better be worth it.

statCheck.pass: Chilling the onion in cold water before cutting helps. The cold slows whatever makes the sting. Not perfect, but better.
statCheck.fail: I don't know any real trick for it. Just speed and tears.

entries.intro: I haven't left my chair in a while and I'm genuinely unsure how long it's been.
details: The sun moved, so it's been at least an hour. Maybe two. The light was on the left side of the room when I sat down.
I wasn't sleeping or thinking about anything in particular. Just sitting.
reactions: Some people would call that wasting time. I'm calling it rest.

entries.intro: There's a patch of sunlight on my floor in the afternoon that the cat from the street always finds.
details: It comes through the front window for about an hour, and every day, there's the cat. I don't feed it or anything.
It leaves when the light moves. Very businesslike about the whole arrangement.
reactions: I respect the commitment. It knows what it wants and it shows up for it.

entries.intro: I sewed a button back on this morning. The third time for the same button.
details: I keep using the same thin thread and it keeps breaking. You'd think I'd learn to use thicker thread.
The shirt's old enough that the fabric around the hole is thinning. The button has less and less to hold onto.
reactions: One more time and I'll retire the shirt. Probably. Maybe.

entries.intro: It's one of those days where every small thing takes longer than it should.
details: The latch stuck, the fire was slow to start, the water took forever to boil. Nothing's wrong, everything's just sluggish.
It's like the whole day decided to move at half speed and didn't tell me.
reactions: I've stopped fighting it. If the day wants to be slow, fine. I'll match it.

statCheck.pass: Damp air does it. Everything swells, friction goes up, and kindling won't catch as quick. The weather sets the day's pace.
statCheck.fail: I don't know why some days drag. They just do.

entries.intro: I found a comfortable spot on the wall to lean against and I've been here ever since.
details: It's in the shade, the stone is cool, and there's just enough of a breeze. I'm not moving.
People keep walking past and I wave. That's as social as I'm getting today.
reactions: I earned this. I don't know what I did to earn it, but I'm claiming it.

entries.intro: The nights are getting noticeably longer. You can feel winter creeping in at the edges.
details: It was dark by the time I finished supper, and I'd started eating early.
The mornings are slow to brighten too. That hour before dawn feels colder and longer than it did a month ago.
reactions: Every year it surprises me, even though it shouldn't.

statCheck.pass: Once you lose more than an hour of daylight, the cold follows fast. The ground loses its stored heat and doesn't get enough sun to replenish it.
statCheck.fail: I just know it gets dark earlier. The why is beyond me.

entries.intro: I walked outside barefoot this morning and the cold grass woke me up better than anything.
details: It's that wet, sharp cold that goes right through you. Ten seconds and I was fully alert.
My feet were numb by the time I got back inside. Worth it, though.
reactions: It's free, it works, and it makes you feel ridiculous. That's a good morning routine.

entries.intro: I've been {self_role} long enough that I can tell a good day from a bad one before breakfast.
details: It's in the little things: how the light hits, how the air feels, whether the morning goes smooth or fights you.
When everything lines up easy, you know to lean into it. Those days don't come every time.
reactions: Today was one of the good ones. I'll take it.

entries.intro: I scrubbed the floor this morning and my knees are killing me.
details: I keep telling myself I'll find a better way to do it, but every time I end up down there on all fours with a brush.
The floor looks good, though. For about a day before the dirt comes back.
reactions: Clean floors and sore knees. The two go together.

entries.intro: I picked up a habit of cracking my knuckles and I can't stop now.
details: I do it without thinking. Halfway through a conversation, my hands just start going one finger at a time.
People have asked me to stop. I've tried. It's like telling yourself not to blink.
reactions: At this point it's part of who I am. An annoying part, but still.

statCheck.pass: It doesn't actually damage anything. It's just air popping in the joint. People think it ruins your hands, but it doesn't.
statCheck.fail: I've heard it's bad for you. I've also heard it's not. I don't care either way.

entries.intro: I spent the afternoon in the shade doing absolutely nothing. Didn't even feel bad about it.
details: There was a list of things I should've done. The list can wait.
I just sat there listening to whatever sounds came by. Wind, voices, birds. No urgency in any of it.
reactions: Some afternoons are for work, and some are for this. Today was for this.

entries.intro: I burned the tip of my finger on the stove this morning. The kind that blisters fast.
details: I stuck it in cold water immediately and held it there for ages. Still throbs when I let go.
It's my index finger, so every single thing I do today reminds me.
reactions: A small burn in a useless spot. That's the worst kind.

entries.intro: I started organizing my storage and got distracted halfway through. Now it's messier than when I started.
details: Everything's pulled out, spread across the floor, sorted into piles that made sense an hour ago. I've lost the thread.
I meant to be done by noon. It's well past that and I'm sitting in the middle of it all.
reactions: I'll finish tomorrow. Or the day after. The piles can wait.

entries.intro: I made enough soup to last three days. The pot's so full I can barely lift it.
details: I always misjudge the portions. What looks like a reasonable amount of vegetables turns into a cauldron's worth.
At least I won't have to cook for a while. Just reheat and eat.
reactions: By day three I'll be sick of it, but right now it feels like planning ahead.

statCheck.pass: Adding a splash of something sharp on day two freshens it up. A little vinegar or citrus juice makes it taste new again.
statCheck.fail: I just reheat it. Same soup, three days running.

entries.intro: My feet were so cold last night I couldn't sleep until I dug out an extra pair of socks.
details: It's amazing how one cold body part can ruin everything. The rest of me was fine. Just the feet.
I tucked them under the blanket, curled up, pulled the edge down. Nothing worked until the socks.
reactions: I'm keeping them by the bed from now on.

entries.intro: I noticed a hole in my pocket today because I lost a coin through it.
details: I heard the clink on the ground and reached into my pocket to find the hole. The coin was already gone.
I found it eventually: it had rolled under a crate. But who knows how many I've lost before noticing.
reactions: Time to mend pockets. Another thing I never thought I'd say about my week.

entries.intro: I lay awake last night listening to the rain on the roof and it was the most content I've been in weeks.
details: There's no better sound when you're warm and dry inside. Just steady, even, no breaks.
I didn't want to fall asleep because I knew I'd stop hearing it.
reactions: Some things are better when you have nowhere to be and nothing to do about them.

entries.intro: I spent twenty minutes untangling a knot in a piece of twine. Twenty minutes I'll never get back.
details: I could have just cut it and used a new piece. But at a certain point, the knot becomes a matter of principle.
I got it in the end. The twine was too short to use by then.
reactions: Some victories aren't worth celebrating. But you still celebrate them.

entries.intro: I went to bed at a reasonable hour, woke up on time, and feel completely rested. It's suspicious.
details: Usually something goes wrong: too hot, too cold, bad dream, strange noise. Last night was just smooth.
I keep waiting for the catch. Good mornings like this don't just happen to me.
reactions: I'll take it and try not to question it too much.

entries.intro: My throat's been scratchy all day. Not sore: just that dry, tickly feeling at the back.
details: I've been drinking tea nonstop trying to soothe it. All it does is make me need to relieve myself constantly.
It's probably nothing. Dry air, dust. The usual suspects.
reactions: If I'm still feeling it tomorrow, I'll worry then. Today I'm just annoyed.

statCheck.pass: Honey in warm water coats the throat better than tea does. The tea actually dries it out more if you drink too much.
statCheck.fail: I don't know what helps. I just keep drinking things and hoping.

entries.intro: I waxed the table today. Haven't done it in over a year.
details: The wood drank it up like it was parched. I ended up using twice as much as I expected.
It's darker now, richer. Looks like a different piece of furniture entirely.
reactions: I keep running my hand over the surface. It's smooth as glass.

entries.intro: I overslept badly today. The sun was well up by the time I opened my eyes.
details: I must have been more tired than I realized. The whole morning routine happened in a rush.
Everything I was supposed to do before noon got pushed to afternoon. The schedule's wrecked.
reactions: Clearly my body had other plans. I just wish it had told my schedule.

entries.intro: The broom handle snapped clean in half this morning. Middle of sweeping.
details: I stood there holding two pieces, just staring at them. That broom was older than most things I own.
I'll bind it with cord for now. It'll look ridiculous but it'll work.
reactions: Nothing lasts forever, but I expected more from a broom handle.

statCheck.pass: If you splint it with a tight wrap of wet cord and let it dry, the cord shrinks and binds tighter than any knot. Stronger than the original.
statCheck.fail: I'll just tie it together and hope. Not much else to do with a broken stick.

entries.intro: I spent the evening just sitting by the fire watching the flames. No reading, no mending, nothing.
details: Fire's endlessly interesting if you let yourself stare at it long enough. The shapes keep changing.
I don't know how long I was there. Long enough for the wood to burn down to coals.
reactions: Sometimes doing nothing is exactly what you're supposed to be doing.

entries.intro: A wasp got into the house today and I spent the better part of an hour dealing with it.
details: It kept bouncing off the window trying to get out. I opened the window and it flew into the other room.
I finally got it out by guiding it with a cloth toward the door. Felt like herding a very angry cloud.
reactions: We both wanted the same thing: for it to be outside. It just took us a while to agree on how.

entries.intro: I restrung the clothesline today. The old one had stretched so much everything sagged into the dirt.
details: Getting it tight enough is the trick. I pulled until my hands were raw and it still had some give.
The shirts actually hang clear of the ground now. Small improvement, big difference.
reactions: It's the kind of fix you only appreciate when you've been living with the problem.

entries.intro: I've had {food_type} three days running. Not because I love it: because I made too much.
details: The first day it was good. The second day it was fine. Today it's penance.
I refuse to throw food away, so I'll keep eating it until it's gone or I am.
reactions: Next time I'll make half as much. I always say that.

entries.intro: The mornings smell like damp earth lately. That heavy, green smell that means the ground is awake.
details: You notice it right when you open the door. Hits you before the cold does.
It's strongest early, before the sun dries everything. By midday it's gone.
reactions: I like it. It smells like things growing.

entries.intro: I bit into something hard in my food today and now my tooth is aching.
details: Don't know what it was: a stone, a seed, a bit of shell. Whatever it was, my molar felt it.
It's that deep, dull ache that comes and goes. Fine one moment, throbbing the next.
reactions: I'll leave it alone and hope it settles. Teeth are not something I want to think about.

entries.intro: I spent the whole morning looking for a tool I just had yesterday. It was in my hand five minutes ago.
details: I retraced my steps three times. Checked every surface, every shelf, every pocket.
Found it on the window ledge behind a jar. How it got there is a mystery I'll never solve.
reactions: I lose more time looking for things than actually using them.

entries.intro: I felt the first real chill of autumn today. Not in the air, but in the ground: you can feel it through your boots.
details: The grass is still green, the leaves haven't turned yet. But the earth knows what's coming.
I noticed I was walking faster without meaning to. The body knows too.
reactions: I like autumn well enough. It's just the knowing where it leads that takes the shine off.

statCheck.pass: The soil cools before the air does. It loses heat from below first, and by the time you feel it in the breeze, the ground's been cold for weeks.
statCheck.fail: I just know it felt cold underfoot. I don't think about the why.

entries.intro: I had a coughing fit this morning that just wouldn't stop. Nothing wrong with me: just swallowed air the wrong way.
details: It went on so long my eyes were watering and my ribs ached from the effort.
Two people stopped to ask if I was all right. I couldn't answer because I was still coughing.
reactions: The human body can be defeated by a sip of water going down the wrong pipe. We're fragile creatures.

entries.intro: I hung a new hook by the door and it's already the most useful thing in the house.
details: I put my coat on it, my bag, my hat. Everything has a place now instead of ending up on the chair.
It took thirty seconds to put up. I don't know why I waited so long.
reactions: The simplest fixes are always the ones you put off longest.

entries.intro: I've been waking up with my arm numb and useless. Sleeping on it wrong.
details: That feeling when the blood starts coming back is awful. Pins and needles from shoulder to fingertips.
I rearrange the pillow every night and by morning I'm right back in the same position.
reactions: My body sleeps how it wants. I've given up trying to correct it.

statCheck.pass: Putting a rolled cloth under the shoulder keeps the arm from tucking underneath you. Takes the pressure off the nerve.
statCheck.fail: I don't know how to stop it. I'm asleep when it happens.

entries.intro: I found a bird's nest tucked under the eave this morning. Tiny, perfectly built, and empty.
details: The birds must have already moved on. The nest is dry and old but still holding together.
It's amazing how something so small can be so well-made. Every twig in place.
reactions: I left it where it was. Maybe they'll come back next year.

entries.intro: I'm running low on candles and the evenings are getting darker. Bad combination.
details: I've been going to bed earlier just to save them. It's either that or sit in the dark.
I rationed the last few: one per evening, no more. Makes you appreciate what light you've got.
reactions: I'll get more soon. But for now, early nights it is.

statCheck.pass: A dish of oil with a floating wick burns longer than a candle and costs less. Not as bright, but it stretches the evening.
statCheck.fail: I don't have a clever alternative. When the candles are gone, the night wins.

entries.intro: I got a splinter under my thumbnail and I can't get it out. It's deep.
details: I've been picking at it with a needle for half an hour. It's in there sideways.
Everything I touch sends a little jolt through the thumb. Turning a latch, buttoning a shirt: agony.
reactions: It'll work its way out eventually. In the meantime, I'm suffering.

entries.intro: Someone was making {food_type} this morning and the smell carried clear across to my door. Incredible.
details: I couldn't tell who or where, but the whole air was thick with it.
It made my own breakfast feel thoroughly inadequate.
reactions: A good smell from someone else's kitchen is one of life's free pleasures.

entries.intro: My chair has finally broken in the way I like. Took years, but the seat fits me perfectly now.
details: There's a slight lean to the left and a dip where I always sit. Nobody else finds it comfortable.
Guests sit in it and shift around trying to find a good spot. I sit down and disappear.
reactions: When this chair goes, I'll mourn it more than furniture deserves.

entries.intro: I stepped outside this morning and the air was so still it felt like the world was holding its breath.
details: No wind, no birds, no sounds at all. Just absolute quiet.
It lasted maybe a minute before the wind picked up and everything started again.
reactions: Those moments are rare enough that you notice them when they happen.

entries.intro: I trimmed the wick on the lantern too short and spent the evening in half-darkness.
details: The flame was barely there: a tiny, flickering thing that threw more shadow than light.
I couldn't read, couldn't mend, couldn't do anything that required seeing clearly.
reactions: I gave up and went to bed early. Some mistakes just end your evening.

entries.intro: I caught a whiff of something rotting in the pantry and spent the afternoon hunting for it.
details: Moved every jar, every sack, checked every corner. Found it at the very back: an onion that had given up on life.
The smell had gotten into the wood. I scrubbed the shelf down twice and it still lingers.
reactions: I'll be checking the back of the pantry more often now.

entries.intro: I have a callus on my palm that's harder than leather. Been building up for months.
details: It's in exactly the spot where the tool handle sits. A perfect imprint of my grip.
It doesn't hurt: if anything, it helps. Like my hand made its own glove.
reactions: It's not attractive, but it's useful. I'll take useful.

statCheck.pass: If you let it build up too thick, it can crack deep and that's worse than a blister. You have to file it down occasionally.
statCheck.fail: I leave it alone. It takes care of itself.

entries.intro: The spring water runs cold enough now to make your teeth ache. That's how you know the season's turning.
details: A month ago it was cool but pleasant. Now it's a shock every morning.
I've started filling the bucket the night before and letting it warm up inside.
reactions: The little adjustments pile up and suddenly you're living a winter routine without meaning to.

statCheck.pass: Groundwater temperature lags the air by weeks. When the water gets this cold, you know the air will follow soon.
statCheck.fail: I don't know why it gets colder at different times. It just does.

entries.intro: I woke up to birdsong so loud it was like they were roosting in my room.
details: Must have been right on the sill. The sound was sharp and close and impossible to sleep through.
I'm not angry at the birds, exactly. They've got their schedule and I've got mine.
reactions: There are worse alarms, I suppose. At least it's seasonal.

entries.intro: I sharpened all my kitchen knives today. Every single one.
details: It's tedious work, but there's a rhythm to it once you get going. The sound of stone on steel is almost pleasant.
The difference is immediate. Everything cuts clean instead of tearing.
reactions: Should have done it weeks ago. Sharp tools make every task faster.

statCheck.pass: The angle matters more than the pressure. Most people push too hard and grind the edge uneven.
statCheck.fail: I just go back and forth until it seems sharp enough. It works.

entries.intro: I walked to the well this morning and forgot the bucket. Just stood there for a second, then walked back.
details: It's a short walk, but doing it twice before breakfast is not how I wanted to start.
The second time I double-checked I had the bucket before leaving. Trust, but verify.
reactions: My head was already three tasks ahead. My body hadn't caught up.

entries.intro: I cooked with garlic this afternoon and now my hands smell like it. Strongly.
details: I've washed them four times. The smell just laughs at soap.
Everything I touch now smells faintly of garlic. My cup, my door handle, my face.
reactions: The food was good, so I consider it a fair trade.

statCheck.pass: Rubbing your hands on steel takes the smell out. A knife blade, a pot handle: anything metal. Don't ask me why it works.
statCheck.fail: I've tried everything. The garlic wins every time.

entries.intro: I sat on the edge of my bed this morning for ten minutes before standing up. Just sat there.
details: Not tired, not dreading anything. Just needed a minute between sleeping and starting.
The light was coming through the shutters in bars. I watched the dust drift through them.
reactions: That in-between time is underrated. Not asleep, not awake. Just existing.

entries.intro: The hinge on the shutters finally gave out. Now one side just hangs there looking defeated.
details: It's been loose for months and I kept tightening it. Today it decided it was done.
The screw holes are stripped so I can't just put it back. I'll have to fill and re-drill.
reactions: Some things can only be tightened so many times before you need a real fix.

statCheck.pass: If I plug the holes with glued dowel and let them set overnight, the new screws will hold like they're in fresh wood.
statCheck.fail: I'm not sure how to fix stripped holes. I'll figure something out.

entries.intro: I spent the evening turning an old cloth into rags. Tearing it into strips by hand.
details: It was a good shirt once. Soft, well-made. But it had too many holes to keep wearing.
There's something final about tearing fabric. Once you start, there's no going back to what it was.
reactions: At least it'll be useful a while longer, just in a different form.

entries.intro: I kept yawning all day and it's not even because I'm tired. My body just decided it was yawning day.
details: I'd finish one yawn and another would start before my jaw was even closed.
At least three people caught it from me. It's the most contagious thing there is.
reactions: I've stopped trying to fight it. The jaw wants what it wants.

entries.intro: I turned my mattress over today. It's like sleeping on a new bed tonight.
details: The underside is firmer, cooler, and doesn't have the dip in the middle where I always lie.
I should do this more often. The amount of difference a flip makes is ridiculous.
reactions: Give it a month and I'll have broken this side in too.

statCheck.pass: If you rotate it end-to-end as well as flipping, it wears more evenly. Most people only flip. The turn makes a bigger difference.
statCheck.fail: I just turn it over. No technique to it.

entries.intro: Clouds moved in fast this afternoon. It was clear one moment and gray the next.
details: I barely had time to get the laundry off the line before the first drops fell.
The temperature dropped with the clouds. You could feel the warmth being pulled out of the air.
reactions: Weather around here doesn't give you much warning. You learn to move fast.

entries.intro: I tripped going up the steps today. Up. Not even the dignity of tripping going down.
details: Caught the toe of my boot on the lip of the stair. My shin connected with the edge on the way down.
It's bruising already. A nice purple line right across the bone.
reactions: At least nobody saw it. Or if they did, they were kind enough to pretend they didn't.

entries.intro: The sunlight this morning had that thick, golden quality that only happens in early autumn.
details: Everything looked warmer and older. Even the plain things looked good in that light.
It only lasts an hour or so before the sun climbs higher and goes back to normal.
reactions: It's the kind of morning that makes you stop and appreciate what's right in front of you.

entries.intro: I ate breakfast in complete silence today. No talking, no noise, nothing. Just the food and me.
details: It was the first time in a while I actually tasted everything. Noticed the salt, the texture, the temperature.
When you eat in silence, you realize how much noise you usually eat through.
reactions: I might start doing that on purpose. It made the whole meal better.

entries.intro: My arms are sore from wringing out wet clothes all morning. Laundry is harder than people think.
details: You twist and squeeze and there's always more water in there. It never feels done.
By the last shirt, I was barely getting any twist out of my wrists.
reactions: At least it's all hanging up now. My arms can recover while the sun does the rest.

statCheck.pass: Rolling the cloth tight around a stick and twisting it wrings out twice as much water with half the effort.
statCheck.fail: I just wring by hand. There's probably a better way, but I don't know it.

entries.intro: I realized today that I've been stirring my porridge the same way my mother did. Same motion, same speed.
details: I don't remember learning it. Must have just absorbed it from watching her every morning as a child.
It's a strange feeling, finding pieces of someone else in the things your hands do without thinking.
reactions: I suppose that's how most things get passed down. Not taught: just repeated until they stick.

statCheck.pass: There's more of my parents in my daily habits than I'd probably admit. The way I fold a cloth, how I stack firewood: all borrowed.
statCheck.fail: I don't think about it much. It's just how I do things.

entries.intro: My pillow smells like the lavender I dried last summer. Must have gotten into the fabric.
details: I hung the bundles in the same room and the scent must have settled into everything.
It's faint: you only catch it when you first lie down. Then it fades, or you stop noticing.
reactions: It's a nice accident. I'll take it over what my pillow usually smells like.

entries.intro: I've been restless all day and I can't point to why. Nothing's wrong, I just can't sit still.
details: I've started and stopped three different tasks. Can't settle into any of them.
My legs want to walk somewhere but I don't have anywhere to go.
reactions: Some days your body has energy your life doesn't have a use for.

entries.intro: The door latch is getting stiff. I have to jiggle it just right or it won't catch.
details: There's a specific angle and amount of force that works. I've got it down to muscle memory.
Anyone else trying to open it just stands there pulling until I come show them the trick.
reactions: I should fix it. But honestly, there's something satisfying about being the only one who can work it.

statCheck.pass: A bit of graphite dust from a pencil rubbed into the mechanism frees it right up. Easier than taking the whole thing apart.
statCheck.fail: I just jiggle it until it works. It's become second nature.

entries.intro: I forgot to eat lunch today. Just forgot. Didn't realize until my stomach started making demands around supper.
details: I was busy with something, I don't even remember what. The day just ran away from me.
By the time I sat down to eat, I was shaking. My hands wouldn't hold steady.
reactions: Skipping meals catches up fast. I won't be doing that again willingly.

entries.intro: There's a patch of wall in my house where the plaster's coming away. Bit by bit, every week.
details: I keep finding little flakes of it on the floor underneath. One day the whole section will come down.
I press on it sometimes to test how solid it still is. The answer is: not very.
reactions: Replastering a wall is one of those jobs that sounds simple and absolutely isn't.

statCheck.pass: Moisture behind the plaster is usually the cause. The wall itself might be fine: it's the seal between the layers that's failed.
statCheck.fail: I don't know why plaster falls off. It just decides it's done.

entries.intro: The first truly warm day after a long cold stretch hit today. Everyone was outside.
details: You could feel people's mood lift. Conversations were louder, walks were longer, nobody was in a hurry.
I stood in the sun for a good five minutes doing nothing but soaking it in.
reactions: We needed that. The cold wears you down more than you realize until the warmth comes back.

entries.intro: My left hand went numb while I was working today. Just dropped what I was holding.
details: Being a {self_role} means gripping the same way for hours. The hand goes dead and you don't notice until something falls.
Shook it out for a minute and it came back, all pins and needles.
reactions: The reminder to shift your grip once in a while. My hand had to shout to get through to me.

entries.intro: I found an old recipe written on a scrap of paper, tucked into the back of a shelf.
details: The handwriting's so faded I can barely make out the amounts. But I recognize the dish.
I think it belonged to whoever lived here before me. The ink has that brown, aged look.
reactions: I might try making it. If I can puzzle out the measurements.

statCheck.pass: Some of the old ingredient names are different from what we use now. 'Sweet earth' is probably sugar, and 'ground stone' is likely salt.
statCheck.fail: Half the words are too faded to read. I'll just guess.

entries.intro: I lay in the grass this afternoon and just watched the clouds. Haven't done that since I was a child.
details: My back was damp by the time I got up, but I didn't care. The sky was too good.
A few people walked past. I'm sure I looked odd, but nobody said anything.
reactions: There's no age limit on lying in the grass. I'd forgotten that.

entries.intro: My calf cramped up in the middle of the night. Woke me from a dead sleep screaming.
details: I had to stand on it to make it release. Stood there in the dark, half asleep, one leg burning.
It's still tender this morning. That deep soreness that stays in the muscle for days.
reactions: Night cramps are the body's cruelest trick. No warning, no cause, just pain.

statCheck.pass: It's usually a sign you haven't been drinking enough water. The muscles tighten when they're short on fluids.
statCheck.fail: I've no idea why it happens. Just does, now and then.

entries.intro: I folded all the laundry the moment it came off the line. Every piece, right away.
details: Usually I pile it on the chair and it lives there for three days. Today I was disciplined.
The closet looks tidy. The chair is empty. I don't trust it to last.
reactions: A rare burst of competence. I'm enjoying it while it lasts.

entries.intro: The air is so dry my lips are cracking. No matter how much water I drink.
details: I've been licking them, which I know makes it worse. Can't help it: they feel like sandpaper.
Even my knuckles are splitting. Everything feels tight and parched.
reactions: I need some kind of salve. Or rain. I'd take either.

statCheck.pass: Beeswax mixed with a drop of oil makes a balm that lasts hours. Better than licking, which actually strips the moisture away.
statCheck.fail: I don't know any remedy. I just lick and suffer.

entries.intro: I heard my own name being called and when I turned around, nobody was there.
details: Clear as anything. My name, one time, from behind me. I spun around and the street was empty.
I'm sure someone was calling for someone else and the wind carried it wrong.
reactions: Gave me a strange feeling for a minute, but I've moved on. Mostly.

entries.intro: I oiled the leather on my belt and boots this evening. Long overdue.
details: The leather was stiff and pale, almost cracking in places. It soaked the oil up eagerly.
Now everything's dark and supple again. Like they remember what they're supposed to feel like.
reactions: Leather lasts forever if you take care of it. The problem is remembering to take care of it.

statCheck.pass: The trick is doing it before the leather dries out completely. Once it cracks, no amount of oil brings it back.
statCheck.fail: I just rub it on when things look dry. Seems to work well enough.

entries.intro: I accidentally knocked over a candle and spent five frantic seconds stamping out the tablecloth.
details: The burn mark is small: about the size of a coin. Could have been much worse.
My heart's still going. That little rush of panic takes a while to fade.
reactions: I'll be more careful where I put the candle from now on. Lesson learned the exciting way.

entries.intro: The days are getting long enough that I have time for a walk after supper again.
details: Last month it was dark by the time I finished eating. Now there's a good hour of light left.
It changes everything about the evening. You feel like the day has more room in it.
reactions: I missed the long evenings more than I realized.

entries.intro: I sneezed so hard this morning that my back seized at the same time. Two disasters at once.
details: I was bent over the table and the sneeze caught me off guard. My whole body clenched.
I spent the next five minutes just standing very still, waiting for everything to unlock.
reactions: The body is not a well-designed machine. It works, but not gracefully.

entries.intro: I swept the path outside my door this morning. Not because it needed it: just because.
details: The rhythm of sweeping is mindless in the best way. Your arms move and your head empties.
I went further than I meant to. Ended up clearing the whole stretch.
reactions: A clean path doesn't change anything, but it looks like someone cares. That counts for something.

entries.intro: There's a draft under the door that I've been ignoring for weeks. Tonight the cold got through.
details: I could feel it on my ankles while I sat by the fire. Cold from below, warm from in front: very confusing.
I stuffed a rolled-up cloth along the gap. Crude, but it stopped the worst of it.
reactions: I'll do a proper fix when the weather demands it. For now, the cloth will hold.

statCheck.pass: A strip of leather nailed to the bottom edge of the door seals the gap better than cloth and survives the damp.
statCheck.fail: I don't know the right way to seal it. The cloth works well enough.

entries.intro: I sat down to mend a sock and ended up mending five. Once you start, you might as well keep going.
details: The first one was the hardest because I had to find the needle and thread. After that it was just repetition.
My stitching is rough, but my feet don't care about aesthetics.
reactions: A productive evening, even if nobody will see the results.

entries.intro: I left the house today and came straight back because I forgot why I left.
details: I stood outside for a second, patted my pockets, looked around, and went back inside.
Remembered thirty seconds later. Of course.
reactions: The house and I are playing a game where I can never fully leave it.

entries.intro: I cleaned under the bed today and found enough dust to stuff a pillow.
details: How does that much accumulate without you noticing? It's not like I never sweep.
There were a few things under there too. A sock, a coin, a scrap of paper I can't read anymore.
reactions: The space under a bed is where lost things go to retire.

entries.intro: My tea has been going cold before I finish it every morning. I keep getting distracted.
details: I make it, set it down, think of one thing I need to do first, and by the time I come back it's lukewarm.
I've tried making it hotter. Doesn't help: I just find bigger distractions.
reactions: I should just drink the tea first and do everything else second. Revolutionary idea.

statCheck.pass: A thicker cup holds heat longer. Thin pottery sheds warmth fast. That's half the problem right there.
statCheck.fail: I don't think about the cup. I just blame myself for getting distracted.

entries.intro: I dropped something heavy on my foot today and I'm limping slightly.
details: Right on the top of the foot where there's no padding at all. Just bone and pain.
I can still walk, but every step has a little wince in it.
reactions: I'd like to say I handled it well. I did not.

entries.intro: I had a conversation today that I've been replaying in my head all evening. Nothing important: just a nice talk.
details: We weren't solving anything. Just standing around talking about nothing in particular.
It's the easy conversations that stay with you. The ones that don't need a point.
reactions: I should do more of that. Just talking for the sake of it.

entries.intro: The floorboards are cold first thing in the morning. Makes getting out of bed harder than it needs to be.
details: I've started keeping a pair of socks right beside the bed so I can put them on before my feet hit the floor.
It's a small thing, but it changed my mornings entirely.
reactions: Comfort is mostly problem-solving the small annoyances. The big ones take care of themselves.

entries.intro: I got a hiccup that wouldn't stop. Thirty minutes of my life, gone to involuntary spasms.
details: I tried holding my breath, drinking water upside down, getting startled. None of it worked.
They just stopped on their own, mid-sentence, as suddenly as they started.
reactions: Your body can hold you hostage and there's nothing you can do about it.

entries.intro: I noticed the light coming through the trees this evening. Late sun through the leaves, all green and gold.
details: I don't usually stop to look at things like that. But it was so vivid I actually paused mid-step.
It only lasted a few minutes before the sun dropped lower and the color drained out.
reactions: Those moments happen every day, probably. Most of the time I'm not paying attention.

entries.intro: I've worn a groove into the path between my door and the well. A proper track in the dirt.
details: Same route, twice a day, every day. The ground just gave up and made a path for me.
When it rains, it turns into a little channel. My own personal stream.
reactions: Routine wears the world down around you, literally.

statCheck.pass: That channel will deepen every rain until it starts washing the soil away. A few flat stones set in the track would fix it before it becomes a problem.
statCheck.fail: I don't think about the dirt. It's just a path.

entries.intro: I dropped an egg on the floor this morning. Watched it happen in slow motion and couldn't stop it.
details: My hand closed on nothing. The egg hit the stone and the mess was instant.
I spent more time cleaning it up than I would have spent cooking it.
reactions: Gone before it could be anything useful. That's a dropped egg for you.

entries.intro: I've been sleeping with the window cracked open and the night air has been perfect.
details: Cool but not cold, with a faint smell of woodsmoke from someone's late fire.
The sounds are different with the window open. You hear the world settling in for the night.
reactions: There's a window of the year when this works. I'm making the most of it.

entries.intro: I've been chewing the inside of my cheek all day without realizing. Now it's raw and sore.
details: It's one of those nervous habits that happens below the level of thought. I only notice when the damage is done.
I'll stop for an hour, and then catch myself doing it again.
reactions: Tomorrow I'll have forgotten about it and start the whole cycle again.

entries.intro: I made the fire just right tonight. Not too big, not too small. The room is exactly the temperature I want.
details: Usually I overshoot and end up sweating, or undershoot and add more wood and overshoot anyway.
Tonight: balance. I'm not touching it. It's perfect and I refuse to ruin it.
reactions: Getting a fire right feels like a genuine accomplishment when you've been getting it wrong all week.

entries.intro: My {self_role} work keeps my hands busy all day. By evening I don't want to hold anything at all.
details: I eat supper and then just sit with my hands open on my knees. Letting them rest.
My grip is weaker by nightfall. Things slip out that wouldn't in the morning.
reactions: A day's work shows in the hands first. The rest of you catches up later.

entries.intro: I tasted the first batch of preserves from last autumn today. Still good.
details: The seal held perfectly and the flavor's actually deepened. Better than when I first made it.
I was nervous opening the jar. You never quite know until you lift the lid.
reactions: Past me did present me a real favor with those jars.

statCheck.pass: The trick is getting the seal airtight before storing. Any gap, any air at all, and the whole jar turns.
statCheck.fail: I just seal them up and hope. It's worked so far.

entries.intro: The wind knocked something over outside last night and I lay there convincing myself not to get up and check.
details: It was a single crash, then silence. No second noise, no movement. Probably a pot or a crate.
I checked this morning and it was the water pail. Rolled halfway across the yard.
reactions: Every noise at night sounds twice as important as it turns out to be.

entries.intro: I cleaned the fireplace grate this morning and my hands are black to the wrists.
details: Soot gets into the creases of your skin and just lives there. I've scrubbed four times.
My fingernails look like I've been digging graves. It'll take days to fully come off.
reactions: A necessary evil. The fire draws better now, so it was worth the filth.

entries.intro: I caught myself whistling today and I don't know where the tune came from.
details: It's not a song I know the name of. Just a few notes that my mouth decided to make on its own.
I've been at it all day. Same four bars, over and over.
reactions: The brain stores music in a place you can't get to on purpose. It just surfaces when it wants.

entries.intro: I woke up convinced I'd overslept and rushed through everything, only to realize I was an hour early.
details: I had breakfast made, boots on, door open, and the sun wasn't even up yet.
So I sat back down and waited. All that urgency for nothing.
reactions: At least I was early for once. Even if it was by accident.

entries.intro: I tried to carry too many things at once and dropped all of them. Every single thing.
details: I had the basket, the jug, and the cloth all balanced in a stack. One wobble and the whole arrangement came apart.
Two trips would have taken less time than picking everything up.
reactions: Laziness and efficiency are not the same thing, no matter how hard I try to make them.

entries.intro: I've been sleeping on the same side for so long that my shoulder has a permanent ache from the pressure.
details: I try sleeping on the other side and it feels wrong. Like wearing someone else's shoes.
My body has its preferred position and it won't negotiate.
reactions: I've accepted the ache. It's part of the deal.

statCheck.pass: A folded cloth between the knees takes pressure off the hip and shoulder. Keeps the spine straight even on your side.
statCheck.fail: I don't know how to fix a sleeping habit. You sleep how you sleep.

entries.intro: I darned a hole in my glove that was letting the cold in through the index finger.
details: One hole, one finger, and somehow it made the entire hand feel frozen.
The patch is thicker than the rest of the glove now. That one finger is actually the warmest.
reactions: If only all problems were that easy to fix and that satisfying when done.

entries.intro: Hail came down for about five minutes this afternoon. Short but violent.
details: The sound on the roof was deafening. Like someone emptying a bucket of stones overhead.
It stopped as fast as it started. Sun came out right after, like nothing happened.
reactions: Weather around here doesn't do anything halfway.

statCheck.pass: Hail that short usually means a fast-moving storm. The anvil top of the cloud throws the ice down hard but passes quick.
statCheck.fail: I don't pretend to understand weather. It does what it wants.

entries.intro: I tightened my belt a notch today. Don't know if I've lost weight or the leather stretched.
details: I'd like to think it's the former. The truth is probably the latter.
Either way, everything fits differently. My trousers stay up without me thinking about it.
reactions: I'll take the small win and not investigate further.

entries.intro: I watched a snail cross the doorstep this morning. The whole journey, start to finish.
details: It took an impressively long time. I almost helped it along, but that felt like cheating.
It left a shining trail behind it on the stone. Still there hours later.
reactions: Sometimes the slowest things are the most interesting to watch.

entries.intro: I cleaned out the gutters today and it was every bit as unpleasant as expected.
details: Wet leaves, mud, things I couldn't identify and didn't want to. All of it came out in handfuls.
My arms are scratched from reaching in at bad angles, and I smell like a swamp.
reactions: Done for the season. I'll forget about it until the water starts overflowing again.

statCheck.pass: If you let the gutters block up, the water backs under the roof edge. That's how you get rot in the fascia boards.
statCheck.fail: I clean them because water drips where it shouldn't when I don't.

entries.intro: I burned my tongue on {food_type}. Again. Because I never learn.
details: I know it's too hot. I can see the steam. I blow on it once and then put it in my mouth anyway.
Now everything will taste muted for two days while my tongue recovers.
reactions: Patience is a virtue I theoretically possess but cannot apply to hot soup.

entries.intro: I stacked the firewood neatly today. Every piece lined up, bark facing out.
details: There's no practical reason for the bark to face out. But it looks better and I stand by it.
The stack's solid enough to lean on. I tested it.
reactions: A tidy woodpile says something about a person. I'm not sure what, but it says something.

statCheck.pass: Bark out actually does help: it sheds rain better and keeps the cut faces dry. So there is a reason.
statCheck.fail: I just like the way it looks. No science to it.

entries.intro: I woke up with a song in my head that I haven't heard in years. Just the chorus, over and over.
details: I can't remember who used to sing it or where I first heard it. Just the melody, stuck on repeat.
I've been humming it all day and it shows no signs of leaving.
reactions: Memory is strange. It holds onto melodies longer than names or faces.

entries.intro: I {self_role} for a living, and the one thing people don't tell you is how much waiting is involved.
details: Waiting for things to dry, cure, settle, cool. Half the job is standing around being patient.
The actual work takes less time than the preparation and the waiting combined.
reactions: I've made peace with it. Patience isn't a talent: it's something you practice whether you like it or not.

entries.intro: I bit into an apple this morning and one of my teeth felt loose. Just for a second.
details: It's fine now. But that instant where you feel something shift in your jaw is deeply unsettling.
I pressed on it with my tongue for the next hour. It hasn't moved again.
reactions: The body likes to send little reminders that nothing is permanent.

entries.intro: I found a spider in my boot this morning. We both had a bad time.
details: I didn't notice until my foot was halfway in. The scramble to get it off was undignified.
The spider ran under the dresser. I checked the other boot three times before putting it on.
reactions: I'll be shaking my boots out every morning for the next month at least.

entries.intro: The butter's gone off. I didn't notice until it was already on the bread.
details: That particular tang hits the back of your tongue a half-second too late. By then you've already swallowed.
I scraped what I could and ate the bread plain. Waste of good bread, honestly.
reactions: I need to be better about checking things before I eat them. But I probably won't be.

entries.intro: I woke up before dawn and the house was so quiet I could hear the walls creaking.
details: Wood expanding and contracting, the roof settling. Sounds you never hear when you're busy.
I just lay there and listened for a while. It was oddly peaceful.
reactions: Sometimes the earliest hours are the most honest. Nothing performing, everything just existing.

entries.intro: My knee pops every time I stand up now. Started a few weeks ago and shows no signs of stopping.
details: It doesn't hurt, exactly: it's more of an announcement that my knee is letting the room know I'm getting up.
People have started looking over when they hear it. That's how loud it's gotten.
reactions: I've stopped being embarrassed and started being resigned. It's just what my knee does now.

statCheck.pass: The pop is usually gas releasing from the joint fluid. Harmless, unless there's pain or swelling with it.
statCheck.fail: I don't know what causes it, probably nothing.

entries.intro: I cooked {food_type} from scratch tonight. Took three times longer than I expected.
details: The preparation alone used every bowl I own. My kitchen looks like a disaster.
But the result was worth it. First bite and I understood why people bother.
reactions: Cooking well takes more patience than I usually have. Tonight was the exception.

entries.intro: I've been rubbing the same knot out of my neck for three days. It won't budge.
details: It's right where the neck meets the shoulder. I can feel it like a small stone under the muscle.
I've tried heat, stretching, and ignoring it. None of those worked.
reactions: It'll go away when it's ready. My body doesn't take instructions from me.

statCheck.pass: A hot cloth held steady for a good fifteen minutes softens the muscle enough to work the knot loose. Most people give up too early.
statCheck.fail: I just dig my thumb into it and hope. Not exactly a method.

entries.intro: The mornings have been foggy. Can't see more than a stone's throw from the door.
details: Everything sounds different in fog. Closer somehow, like the world has shrunk.
It burns off by midmorning, but those first few hours feel like you're living inside a cloud.
reactions: I don't mind it. There's something calming about a small world.

entries.intro: I cut my finger peeling vegetables and bled all over the cutting board.
details: It wasn't deep, but fingers bleed like you've been stabbed. Very dramatic for a minor injury.
Wrapped it in a cloth and finished the peeling one-handed. Slower, but it got done.
reactions: The vegetables don't care about your injuries. They still need peeling.

entries.intro: I sat on the step this evening and watched the bats come out at dusk. Dozens of them.
details: They pour out of somewhere all at once, wheeling around in the half-light. Fast little things.
You can barely track them against the darkening sky. They appear and disappear between blinks.
reactions: Free entertainment, every evening. Just have to remember to look up.

entries.intro: I tripped on my own doorstep this morning. The one I cross twice a day without incident.
details: Caught my toe on the lip and staggered forward like I'd been shoved. Nobody saw, thank goodness.
I've walked over that step a thousand times. My foot just decided today was the day to forget.
reactions: Familiarity breeds carelessness, apparently.

entries.intro: My hands are rough enough to snag cloth. I ran my palm over the tablecloth and heard it catch.
details: Calluses on top of calluses. I couldn't feel a coin through my fingertips if I tried.
I remember when my hands were soft. Barely, but I remember.
reactions: Working hands aren't pretty, but they're honest.

entries.intro: The first frost came last night. Everything had a white edge to it this morning.
details: The grass crunched underfoot and the air had that sharp bite to it. Winter's coming whether we're ready or not.
The water pail outside had a thin skin of ice on top. I cracked it with my knuckle.
reactions: I like the first frost. It's the twentieth one that wears you down.

entries.intro: I had the best sleep of my life last night, no idea why. Just woke up feeling perfect.
details: Usually I'm stiff, groggy, reluctant. This morning I was just awake and ready, like something clicked.
I keep trying to figure out what I did differently so I can repeat it. Nothing comes to mind.
reactions: A gift sleep: you don't earn them and you can't summon them, you just appreciate them.

entries.intro: I've been yawning all day. Can't stop. Every few minutes another one hits.
details: My jaw's getting sore from it. The muscles around my ears ache from the stretch.
The worst part is it's contagious. I yawn and whoever I'm talking to yawns and then I yawn again.
reactions: I slept fine. My body just decided today is a yawning day.

entries.intro: I organized the shelf above the fireplace. Everything in its place for once.
details: Took everything down, wiped the shelf, put it all back in a sensible order.
It looks like someone competent lives here. I'm as surprised as anyone.
reactions: Give it a week. It'll be chaos again. But for now, it's satisfying.

entries.intro: My hip's been aching since yesterday. A deep, dull sort of ache that won't let you forget it's there.
details: Sitting makes it worse, standing makes it worse, walking helps for a minute and then makes it worse.
I've been shifting position every few minutes like I can't get comfortable. Because I can't.
reactions: It'll pass. These things always do. They just take their time about it.

entries.intro: I overcooked the eggs again. Hard as stones.
details: I stepped away for just a moment. One moment. Came back and they were little rubber balls.
I ate them anyway. They don't taste bad: they just resist being eaten.
reactions: Eggs don't forgive a lapse in attention. Not even a short one.

entries.intro: The sunset was extraordinary this evening. Red and gold, the whole sky on fire.
details: I stood there with my mouth open like I'd never seen a sunset before. But this one was different.
Even people who don't normally look up stopped and stared for a minute.
reactions: The sky puts on a show like that and charges nothing for admission.

entries.intro: I woke up with a crick in my wrist. Must have slept on it at an odd angle.
details: I can rotate it one direction but not the other. Everything I pick up I have to think about.
It's the kind of minor injury that makes every task take twice as long.
reactions: A reminder that you don't appreciate your wrist until it stops cooperating.

entries.intro: I've had a pebble in my shoe for the last hour and I keep telling myself I'll stop and get it out. I haven't.
details: It migrates around with every step. Sometimes it's tolerable, sometimes it's right under the heel.
Taking a boot off and putting it back on is just enough effort that I keep walking.
reactions: Stubbornness and laziness fighting to a draw, at the expense of my foot.

entries.intro: I sharpened every knife in the kitchen tonight. All of them, even the ones I barely use.
details: The whetstone makes that sound that goes right through you. But the result is worth it.
I tested the edge on a scrap of paper. Clean cut. Very satisfying.
reactions: A sharp knife is safer than a dull one. That's what I tell myself to justify the hour I spent.

statCheck.pass: The angle matters more than the pressure. Most people push too hard and grind the edge flat instead of sharpening it.
statCheck.fail: I just scrape it along the stone until it feels sharp. Works for me.

entries.intro: I drank the last of the water before bed and was too tired to refill it. Now I'm paying for that.
details: Woke up in the middle of the night with a dry mouth and nothing to drink. Had to go to the well in the dark.
The cold air woke me up fully, so getting back to sleep was its own challenge.
reactions: Night-me always betrays morning-me. It's a consistent pattern.

entries.intro: A warm breeze came through this afternoon. The kind that smells like rain but doesn't deliver.
details: The sky darkened for an hour and then cleared without a drop falling. All promise, no follow-through.
The air's been heavy and humid since. Sticky sort of feeling that clings to your skin.
reactions: I would have preferred the rain, honestly. At least that settles things.

entries.intro: I made my bed this morning. First time in a while, and I'm unreasonably proud of it.
details: The blankets are pulled tight, the pillow's centered, the whole thing looks like it belongs to someone organized.
I stood back and looked at it for longer than any reasonable person should look at a made bed.
reactions: Starting the day with one completed task tricks the brain into thinking you have your life together.

entries.intro: I've been a {self_role} long enough that I can tell the season by how my joints feel.
details: Damp weather settles into the knuckles first. Cold goes for the knees. Dry heat's the shoulders.
My body's become a weather instrument. Not a very precise one, but reliable.
reactions: I used to look outside to check the weather. Now I just wake up and listen to the complaints.

statCheck.pass: The old-timers used to say the ache comes before the storm, not during it. Something about the pressure dropping before the rain arrives.
statCheck.fail: I don't know the science. I just know when my knees hurt, it's going to rain.

entries.intro: I woke up to rain on the roof this morning. Lay there for a good ten minutes just listening.
details: Heavy at first, then easing to a steady patter. The kind of sound that makes you never want to move.
I had things to do, but the rain made a strong case for staying exactly where I was.
reactions: Rain is either the worst sound or the best sound, depending on whether you have to go out in it.

entries.intro: I found a bruise on my shin and I have absolutely no memory of hitting it on anything.
details: It's a proper bruise too. Dark and tender. Whatever I walked into, I walked into it hard.
I've been retracing my steps all morning trying to figure out what I hit. Nothing comes to mind.
reactions: Phantom bruises are the body's way of saying you're not paying attention to your own life.

entries.intro: The bread I baked today actually rose properly. I nearly shouted when I saw it.
details: Usually it comes out dense enough to use as a doorstop. Today it was light, golden, the crust cracked just right.
I tore into it while it was still warm and ate half the loaf standing at the table.
